question: Which component stores the entire state of a Kubernetes cluster as a distributed key-value store?
explanation: etcd is the strongly-consistent distributed key-value store used by the control plane to persist all API objects and cluster state.

question: Which component decides which node a newly-created Pod should run on?
explanation: The kube-scheduler watches for unscheduled Pods, filters feasible nodes, scores them, and assigns the Pod to the best node.

question: Which control-plane component is the single entry point for all REST interactions with the cluster?
explanation: The kube-apiserver exposes the Kubernetes REST API and is the front-end of the control plane; every other component talks through it.

question: Which component runs control loops that continuously reconcile the actual state of the cluster with the desired state recorded in the API?
explanation: kube-controller-manager runs built-in controllers (ReplicaSet, Deployment, Node, Endpoints, ServiceAccount, etc.) that reconcile state through the API server.

question: Which agent runs on every worker node and is responsible for starting containers described by PodSpecs?
explanation: The kubelet is the primary node agent. It registers the node, watches the API server for PodSpecs, and instructs the container runtime to run containers.

question: Which component programs Linux iptables/ipvs rules on nodes so that Service virtual IPs route to the correct Pods?
explanation: kube-proxy runs on each node and maintains network rules that implement Service abstractions, forwarding traffic to the right backend Pods.

question: Which component integrates Kubernetes with an underlying cloud provider API (for load balancers, node lifecycle, routes, etc.)?
explanation: cloud-controller-manager isolates cloud-specific logic so that vendors can evolve integrations independently of the core project.

question: What is the default authorization mode on a vanilla upstream kube-apiserver (no flags set)?
explanation: The upstream default is AlwaysAllow. Production installers (kubeadm, managed distributions) override this to Node,RBAC.

question: A developer runs `kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml`. Which component first receives the request?
explanation: All client requests go through the kube-apiserver; only it reads and writes etcd directly.

question: A Pod exists in the cluster but never schedules. The user sees the Pod stuck in Pending with no Node. Which component is most likely the cause?

options.label: a
options.text: kubelet on the target node is down

options.label: b
options.text: kube-scheduler is down or cannot find a feasible node

explanation: A Pod remains Pending when no Node is assigned. That is the scheduler's job; either the scheduler is unhealthy or no node satisfies constraints/taints/resources.

question: Which statement about etcd is TRUE?

options.label: a
options.text: etcd is optional and can be replaced with PostgreSQL

options.label: b
options.text: etcd serves reads and writes directly to kubectl clients

options.label: c
options.text: etcd is a strongly-consistent distributed key-value store accessed only through kube-apiserver

options.label: d
options.text: etcd provides service discovery by resolving DNS names for Pods

explanation: etcd backs the API server and must remain strongly consistent. Clients never talk to etcd directly; only kube-apiserver does.

question: How many etcd members are recommended in a highly-available control plane to tolerate one failure?
explanation: etcd uses Raft quorum (N/2 + 1). Three members tolerate one failure; four provide no more fault tolerance than three while costing more write latency, so odd numbers are preferred.

question: Which of the following is NOT a control-plane component?
explanation: kubelet runs on every worker node, not on the control plane. The others are core control-plane components.

question: Which controller inside kube-controller-manager is responsible for creating replacement Pods when a Node becomes NotReady?
explanation: The NodeLifecycle (Node) controller monitors node health, taints unhealthy nodes, and triggers eviction so higher-level controllers (Deployment/ReplicaSet) can place replacements.

question: Which component is responsible for cluster DNS inside a standard Kubernetes cluster?
explanation: CoreDNS is the default in-cluster DNS server, deployed as a Deployment and exposed through a kube-dns Service.

question: A workload calls another Service by its DNS name. Which node component turns the Service ClusterIP into a real Pod IP at the packet level?
explanation: CoreDNS returns the Service ClusterIP; kube-proxy rewrites packets (via iptables/ipvs/nftables) to land on one of the backing Pods.

question: Which statement about the Kubernetes API is TRUE?

options.label: a
options.text: It is a binary gRPC protocol not usable from curl

options.label: b
options.text: It is a RESTful HTTP API organized into API groups and versions

options.label: c
options.text: It is proprietary and only accessible through kubectl

explanation: The Kubernetes API is HTTP+JSON (with optional Protocol Buffers) organized into groups (core, apps, batch, etc.) and versions (v1, v1beta1, v1alpha1).

question: What mechanism lets users extend the Kubernetes API with new object kinds without forking the API server?
explanation: CRDs register new kinds with the API server. Combined with a controller, they implement the Operator pattern.

question: Which statement best describes a Kubernetes controller?

options.label: a
options.text: A CLI tool that reads YAML files

options.label: b
options.text: A control loop that watches the API for a resource kind and reconciles actual state toward desired state

options.label: c
options.text: A plugin for the container runtime

options.label: d
options.text: A replacement for systemd on the node

explanation: The controller pattern is central to Kubernetes: watch for changes, compare actual vs desired state, take corrective action, repeat.

question: Which component is NOT part of the data plane of a Kubernetes cluster?
explanation: kube-scheduler is a control-plane component. kubelet, kube-proxy, and the container runtime run on worker nodes and form the data plane.

question: Which admission mechanism intercepts API requests after authentication/authorization but before the object is persisted to etcd?
explanation: Admission controllers (mutating and validating) run inside the API server between authorization and persistence, enforcing policies and defaulting values.

question: Why must all control-plane components talk through kube-apiserver rather than directly to etcd?

options.label: b
options.text: To centralize authentication, authorization, admission, and schema validation of all writes

options.label: d
options.text: To avoid installing etcd client libraries on every component

explanation: The API server is the only component that should read/write etcd. Routing through it enforces access control, admission, schema validation, and a consistent watch stream.

question: Which command prints the health of core control-plane components?
explanation: Core components usually run as static Pods in kube-system. Listing the namespace surfaces their status quickly.

question: What is a static Pod?

options.label: a
options.text: A Pod managed directly by the kubelet from a local manifest file, not via the API server

options.label: c
options.text: A Pod on a read-only filesystem

options.label: d
options.text: A Pod created from a StatefulSet

explanation: The kubelet watches a directory (e.g., /etc/kubernetes/manifests) and runs any Pod spec it finds there. Control-plane components are often deployed this way.

question: Which statement about leader election among control-plane replicas is TRUE?

options.label: a
options.text: kube-apiserver elects a single leader that handles all writes

options.label: b
options.text: kube-controller-manager and kube-scheduler use leader election so only one active replica acts at a time

options.label: c
options.text: Leader election is performed by kubelet

options.label: d
options.text: etcd does not need leader election

explanation: Multiple replicas of kube-controller-manager and kube-scheduler run for availability; leader election (via a Lease) ensures only one is active. kube-apiserver is stateless and load-balanced.
